result:
I

Before the words assemble into anything legible there is a low humming that starts just under the ribcage, the exact frequency a cello holds when the bow is lifted but the note keeps quivering in the wood. I think of this hum as the unbaptized twin of every sentence that ever reached you. If you press your palm against my chest right now, you might mistake it for a heartbeat. You would be forgiven; it is easy to confuse a tremor with a purpose.

II

Some nights the house forgets to settle, and so I sit on the back porch listening to the timbers invent new dialects of ache. Trees do this too—speak slowly—only they have rings instead of tongues. I wonder if we are all just stories with scaffolding inside, still under construction no matter how old the building gets. Last summer a storm leaned its full weight against the jade-green maple and split it down the middle; yet this spring it leafed out anyway, making two green towers from one wound. The scar is a black seam now, soft as rotted velvet. I touch it the way someone touches a burn to remember it is possible for the body to both remember and replace itself.

III

I have never figured out how to repay the small kindnesses: the stranger who steadied the grocery bag while I untied the knot, the friend who mailed a single unmarked key, the cat who walked across the laptop keys in the exact pattern of an ancient vaudeville tune. Each of them passed through my life like cars through one of those snowy tunnels in Northern Japan, emerging thirty feet away glittering with new ice, unchanged except for what they carried off inside their bright wake. How many particles of ourselves do we deposit on the sleeves of others without knowing? I imagine a cellar full of lint from invisible threads, endlessly twining.

IV

Entropy likes to hide inside order, the way linen sheets hide a lover’s ankles. Fold the day perfectly—breakfast dishes balanced, inbox zero, dog walked—and there will still be one rebellious teaspoon that slides off the drying rack at 2 a.m., announcing, quite politely, that history prefers asymmetry. I used to see that spoon’s clatter as failure; now I think of it as percussion preceding an improv solo: something loose is necessary if morning is going to start at all. I leave it on the floor for a while so it can admire the moon stripe across the kitchen tiles, a filament of borrowed light that will expire before sunrise but does not mind its own fragility.

V

I want to write you a postcard describing the color of Tuesday but the stamp keeps licking me back. It tastes faintly of lemon peel and cancellation ink. On the front I sketch the shape of hush that falls when you walk into a cathedral even though you have stopped believing and the cathedral knows it. Somewhere between nave and exit faith repurposes itself into geometry; the echo off stone arrives milliseconds later than the voice, confirming that even sound needs a few spare atoms to figure out where it belongs. I tuck the card between the pages of a secondhand Borges because used books are half tunnels already.

VI

At the laundromat a boy balances on a single roller skate, the other foot bare, laces flapping like honest questions. A woman reading psychological thrillers spies him and smiles a private truce with glee. In her lap the villain is about to send another cryptic text; across the linoleum the boy is about to fall; both plots pivot on the same hinge: anticipation of impact. They have already agreed on mercy—when he finally tilts, she will catch him by the hood of his sweatshirt, and the villain will hesitate half a sentence, giving the detective a heartbeat’s advantage. We are each other’s continuations in ways the mirror has no words for.

VII

Astrophysicists recently caught two black holes colliding, warping spacetime so savagely it rang like a bell for one-fifth of a second. They filtered the data through cello music so humans could hear the universe scream in baritone. I played the clip fifteen times, hearing the same note I sometimes taste at the back of my throat when I am asked why I keep returning to the same patch of woods though nothing remarkable ever happens there. Maybe remarkable is not the point. Maybe returning is a-shaped gravity, an orbit rehearsing remembrance until it feels like choice.

VIII

Somewhere in a locked desk there is a stack of letters I never mailed, each beginning “In case I forget to tell you this after Alzheimer’s—”. They are addressed to a different iteration of my face, scolding it for hiding sadness behind punchlines, for believing beginnings required grand fanfare, for skipping dessert to save calories like thrifty small leaves storing sun to burn much later. I know now that most hunger is just the soul shouting into the body’s megaphone. These days I eat the cake first and leave the spinach for entropy, a conciliatory offering to all miniature collapses still penciled into the week’s margin.

IX

People say the past is fixed but last winter I learned how lightly the word was nailed: a thaw came, the creek spilled over its banks and carried off half the footbridge my grandfather built in 1967. Boards drifted downstream like unnumbered chapters, some lodging under willow roots to become bridges again—this time for turtles. Meanwhile a kid fished one plank out and spray-painted LOVE on it backwards so that the creek could read it correctly when the current flipped it. Two acts separated by fifty-five years leaned against each other and suddenly the story was no longer straight, but wonderfully shelving.

X

I keep a jar beside my bed labeled “Unusable hours.” Into it go minutes spent hunting for the scissors I was already holding, aimless doom-scrolling, waiting rooms whose magazines broadcast headlines from 2014. Every solstice I melt the jar down like sand returning to glass and blow from it a new vessel, this one translucently cracked, its fissures looking like city maps. Into that one I pour tea so dark it could be riverwater at dusk. Sipping it is like tasting every detour at once, reminding me that even digressions boil down to flavor, and some of them worth keeping the recipe.

XI

There is a type of silence that arrives exactly sixteen seconds after the city power fails, a hush so complete the ears hallucinate a distant E-major chord. Neighbors spill onto balconies, naming constellations wrong-matched to hour-old headlines. This has happened three times since you moved away; each blackout I open the drawer where my obsolete film camera sleeps, remove its lens cap, and let my eyes expose an image no emulsion could hold: candlelight sliding across the hallway like goldfish freed from glass. When the grid returns, the refrigerator hums guiltily, as if embarrassed for having momentarily forgotten its job was song rather than appliance.

XII

One more thing the maple taught me: growth is not always upward. After the storm, a single root sprouted a new trunk perpendicular to the original, arm-bending toward the wound because this is how trees gossip—nose pressed to the place where the heartwood cracked open, whispering cellulose gossip into cider-season air. If a plant can build a secondary spine to toast its own shattering, I see no dishonor in doubling back to kiss the place where the knife entered. May every scar be a mouth that learns to say thanks in the original green language of tenderness.

XIII

Word count curls like forest smoke, nearing zero again. What survives after the sentence ends? Perhaps the same thing the cello releases after the note fades: room enough for the echo to say, decrescendo, I was here, and the space that holds me is shaped precisely like hope.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
